verdict.reason: Skip this generic supernatural romance that wastes its promising cast on tired wish-fulfillment horror tropes. The HDR presentation cannot save weak writing that turns a potentially fun premise into predictable cautionary tale mechanics.

ratings.plot: After breaking the mysterious “One Wish Willow” to win his crush’s heart, a hopeless romantic finds himself getting exactly what he asked for, but soon discovers that some desires come at a dark, sinister price. Directed by Curry Barker. Starring Michael Johnston, Inde Navarrette, Cooper Tomlinson, Megan Lawless, and Andy Richter.

verdict.reason: Skip this generic supernatural romance that squanders its dark fairy tale premise. The movie party format cannot save a film that feels like a Hallmark horror knockoff with Andy Richter inexplicably thrown into the cast.

Skip unless you need to see everything first or the premium format adds real value. Early access screenings often lack the energy of a proper theatrical run and this title doesn't warrant jumping the line.

ratings.plot: Less than 24 hours into his parole, charismatic thief Danny Ocean is already rolling out his next plan: In one night, Danny's hand-picked crew of specialists will attempt to steal more than $150 million from three Las Vegas casinos. But to score the cash, Danny risks his chances of reconciling with ex-wife, Tess.

verdict.reason: This is the rare heist film that makes every beat look effortless when it's actually clockwork precision. Soderbergh's direction turns Vegas into a character and the ensemble cast into a jazz band where everyone knows exactly when to solo.

ratings.plot: The struggle of women in a country that excludes them from entering stadiums.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's great acts of defiance disguised as sports comedy. Panahi shot this guerrilla-style outside Tehran's Azadi Stadium during an actual World Cup qualifier, turning real risk into electric filmmaking.

ratings.plot: A young girl discovers the truth about her family's seemingly spontaneous road trip in this poignant drama starring John Magaro (Past Lives).

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want to catch a promising new voice before the festival buzz fades. Magaro's presence elevates what could have been another indie road trip into something more grounded.

ratings.plot: An ex-military convict seeks a fresh start in a small town, but his past catches up as he finds love amid danger and chaos.

verdict.reason: Worth checking out if RZA's directorial work interests you beyond his music career. The small-town redemption setup gives him room to explore character work over his usual stylized action.

Skip unless you're drawn to experimental narrative structures that prioritize mood over conventional storytelling. The film's deliberate pacing and abstract approach to the Orpheus myth rewards patience but lacks the urgency that makes theatrical viewing essential.

ratings.plot: A comedy (sort of) about young men in America.

verdict.reason: Promising debut worth supporting if you are curious about new voices. Boyson shows potential but the execution does not justify theatrical urgency.

ratings.plot: A journey over the walls that divide us, to explore the age-old debate of land and power, amid the fight for greater access to nature.

verdict.reason: Strong documentary filmmaking but lacks the cinematic scope that demands big screen viewing. Wallace delivers solid journalism about land access issues that works just as well at home.

ratings.plot: The definitive documentary on the New York School Painters. Featuring footage of all the major figures of the New York Art Scene between 1940-1970, showing many of the artists before they became famous.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for understanding the New York School and worth seeing these artists speak in their own words before fame changed everything. De Antonio captures Pollock, de Kooning, and Warhol with the unvarnished directness that made him the definitive documentary filmmaker of his generation.

ratings.plot: American military personnel interact warily with a variety of Italian locals over a year and a half in the push north during the Italian Campaign of WWII as German forces make their retreat.

verdict.reason: Essential Rossellini at his peak - catch this neorealist masterpiece on the big screen. The episodic structure builds devastating emotional power through accumulation rather than conventional plotting.

ratings.plot: A divorced woman and her diabetic daughter take refuge in their newly-purchased house's safe room when three men break-in, searching for a missing fortune.

verdict.reason: Skip it unless you need a Fincher fix tonight. The director's technical precision elevates what amounts to a feature-length home invasion exercise.

ratings.plot: After a young couple witnesses a gruesome highway accident, they soon realize they did not leave the crash scene alone, as a demonic presence called the Passenger won't stop until it claims them both.

verdict.reason: Decent possession horror but nothing urgent about catching it theatrically. Øvredal knows his craft but the scares work just as well at home.
Skip this documentary that fails to capture the magic of its legendary subject. The film treats football's greatest player like a Wikipedia entry instead of exploring what made him transcendent.

ratings.plot: Following a man's suicide, time traverses back to reveal six chapters of his life on why he committed suicide.

verdict.reason: Lee Chang-dong's reverse chronology masterpiece rewards theatrical attention. The backwards structure creates devastating emotional impact that builds with each revelation.

ratings.plot: Wendy and her brothers are whisked away to the magical world of Neverland with the hero of their stories, Peter Pan.
ratings.director: Clyde Geronimi, Wilfred Jackson, Hamilton Luske

verdict.reason: Disney's golden age animation deserves the big screen treatment for its fluid character movement and rich Technicolor backgrounds. The flying sequences lose their magic on home viewing compared to theatrical projection.

ratings.plot: Steve Freeling lives with his wife, Diane, and their three children, Dana, Robbie, and Carol Anne, in Southern California where he sells houses for the company that built the neighborhood. It starts with just a few odd occurrences, such as broken dishes and furniture moving around by itself. However, when he realizes that something truly evil haunts his home, Steve calls in a team of parapsychologists led by Dr. Lesh to help before it's too late.

verdict.reason: Essential horror that belongs on the biggest screen you can find. Hooper's practical effects and sound design create visceral scares that streaming can't replicate.

ratings.plot: A suburban housewife's world falls apart when she finds that her pornographer husband is serially unfaithful to her, her daughter is pregnant, and her son is suspected of being the foot-fetishist who's been breaking local women's ...

verdict.reason: Essential Waters at his most accessible without losing his transgressive edge. The Odorama gimmick turns a theatrical screening into a proper event you cannot replicate at home.

movies.title: Portrait of a Lady on Fire

ratings.plot: On an isolated island in Brittany at the end of the eighteenth century, a female painter is obliged to paint a wedding portrait of a young woman.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone who believes cinema can capture the intensity of unspoken desire. Sciamma builds every frame around the act of looking, making the theater the perfect place to experience her controlled visual intimacy.

ratings.plot: A woman starts exhibiting increasingly disturbing behavior after asking her husband for a divorce. Suspicions of infidelity soon give way to something much more sinister.

verdict.reason: Zulawski's unhinged masterpiece demands big screen viewing for maximum impact. Adjani's performance is one of the most intense ever captured on film.

ratings.plot: Leopoldo is an 11 year old boy who grew up surrounded by artists. During the three months of spring, the boy discovers love in one of his of poetry classmates which she then leaves him with a broken heart.

verdict.reason: Skip this gentle coming-of-age tale that never justifies a theatrical visit. Giralt's modest direction works better as a quiet home viewing than a night out at the movies.

movies.title: PRIVATE EVENT TODAY IN THEATER & COMMISSARY

verdict.reason: No public screening means no ticket to buy. Check back tomorrow for regular programming.

ratings.plot: A science teacher wakes up alone on a spaceship. As his memory returns, he uncovers a mission to stop a mysterious substance killing Earth's sun and that an unexpected friendship may be the key.

verdict.reason: Lord and Miller bring their inventive visual storytelling to hard science fiction in a way that demands the big screen experience. The 156-minute runtime lets them build both the cosmic stakes and the unlikely friendship that anchors the story.

movies.title: Pusher II: With Blood on my Hands
Pusher III: I’m the Angel of Death

ratings.plot: A nostalgic look at radio's golden age focusing on one ordinary family and the various performers in the medium.

verdict.reason: Charming but minor Woody Allen that works fine at home. The episodic structure and nostalgic tone play better as comfort viewing than essential cinema.
Skip this title with no available cast, plot, or production details. The complete lack of information makes theater attendance pointless.

ratings.plot: A bored photographer recovering from a broken leg passes the time by watching his neighbors and begins to suspect one of them of murder.

verdict.reason: Essential Hitchcock that demands the big screen experience. The film's entire architecture depends on you sharing Stewart's confined viewpoint, which only works in a theater.

ratings.plot: Through unlikely bonds formed during overnight shifts at a local aquarium, Tova, an elderly widow, learns of a life-changing discovery that may bring her joy and wonder once again.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want a gentle crowd-pleaser about human connection, but this aquarium drama probably works fine at home. Newman directs with care, but the story feels more suited to streaming than theatrical urgency.

A retrospective screening worth your time if you're ready for Rossellini's deliberate pacing and moral complexity. The theatrical experience depends entirely on which specific film is showing and the quality of the print.

ratings.plot: N/A

verdict.reason: Wait for more information before buying a ticket tonight. The debut feature from co-directors Lengol and Mobasser lacks enough known details to justify the gamble on an unproven theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: RZA’S ONE SPOON OF CHOCOLATE follows Unique (Shameik Moore, DOPE and voice of Miles Morales in the Spider-Verse films), a veteran and ex-convict seeking a fresh start in a small town. After an altercation with a gang of locals, he starts to suspect they may have something to do with the disappearance of young men in the area – including his cousin. As he digs for the truth, he finds himself the target of not only the gang but the local sheriff’s office, whose involvement in the disappearances may be even more sinister. Instead of waiting for his turn to be picked off, Unique and those closest to him (Paris Jackson, RJ Cyler) decide to fight back in this sharply satirical and stylish action thriller from the visionary leader of the Wu-Tang Clan.

verdict.reason: Essential for anyone interested in genre filmmaking that tackles social issues with style and bite. RZA proves his directorial chops extend beyond martial arts pastiche, crafting a thriller that uses horror tropes to examine real community trauma.

movies.title: Salo, or the 120 Days of Sodom

ratings.plot: Young blind Sarah is staying with relatives in their English countryside manor. An unknown maniac enters the house and murders all of Sarah's relatives. When the culprit realizes that Sarah is still alive, he pursues her.

verdict.reason: Fleischer delivers solid craftsmanship in a basic cat-and-mouse premise that works better as a rental. The blind protagonist angle adds tension but the 89-minute thriller lacks the theatrical punch to justify the trip.

ratings.plot: She's the perfect all-American parent: a great cook and homemaker, a devoted recycler, and a woman who'll literally kill to keep her children happy.

verdict.reason: John Waters's suburban satire plays best with an audience that can laugh at Kathleen Turner's perfectly calibrated performance. The dark comedy timing needs the communal theater experience.

ratings.plot: A young Shaolin follower reunites with his discouraged brothers to form a soccer team using their martial art skills to their advantage.

verdict.reason: Stephen Chow's kinetic blend of wire-fu and sports comedy deserves the big screen treatment. The digital effects that looked cheap in 2001 now play as deliberately cartoonish in the best possible way.

ratings.plot: When her brother decides to ditch for a couple weeks, Viola heads over to his elite boarding school, disguised as him, and proceeds to fall for his school's star soccer player, and soon learns she's not the only one with romantic ...

verdict.reason: Worth a watch if you need a comfort-food gender-swap comedy that actually commits to its Shakespearean source. Amanda Bynes carries the entire film with genuine comic timing that elevates the familiar formula.
Worth it if you want to sample emerging voices in a festival setting. Short film programs live or die by curation quality and this one lacks enough standout pieces.

movies.title: Shorts 2: Go Back and Get It

verdict.reason: Better than most short film collections but still uneven across its runtime. The strongest pieces justify the weaker ones if you have patience for the format.
Skip unless you specifically want to support short film programming. The thematic coherence is loose and only half the films earn their screen time.

ratings.plot: A mysterious young drifter who calls herself Nomi Malone hitches a ride to Las Vegas, where she finds work as a stripper and sets about clawing her way to the top of the Vegas showgirls.

verdict.reason: Verhoeven's masterpiece of American excess disguised as exploitation trash. The theatrical experience amplifies both the absurdity and the genuine critique of Vegas capitalism.

ratings.plot: Winner of the first Academy Award for Best Animated Feature, SHREK sparked a motion picture phenomenon and captured the world's imagination with the Greatest Fairy Tale Never Told! Relive every moment of Shrek's daring quest to rescue the feisty Princess Fiona with the help of his loveable, loudmouthed Donkey and win back the deed to his beloved swamp.

verdict.reason: Skip the standard screening when the movie party version offers the same film with added energy. Without the communal experience, this plays better at home where you can pause for snacks during the slower middle act.

ratings.plot: Winner of the first Academy Award for Best Animated Feature, SHREK sparked a motion picture phenomenon and captured the world's imagination with the Greatest Fairy Tale Never Told! Relive every moment of Shrek's daring quest to rescue the feisty Princess Fiona with the help of his loveable, loudmouthed Donkey and win back the deed to his beloved swamp.

verdict.reason: Worth it only if you want to relive the first film that proved animated movies could be smart and subversive for adults. The movie party atmosphere might enhance the jokes you have not heard in twenty years, but the film streams everywhere.

ratings.plot: Everything that farmer Graham Hess (Mel Gibson) assumed about the world is changed when he discovers a message - an intricate pattern of circles and lines - carved into his crops. As he investigates the unfolding mystery, what he finds will forever alter the lives of his brother (Joaquin Phoenix) and children (Rory Culkin), (Abigail Breslin). A unique story that explores the mysterious real-life phenomena of crop signs and the effects they have on one man and his family.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want Shyamalan before he lost his way, but the alien invasion mechanics fall apart under scrutiny. Gibson and Phoenix anchor the family drama with genuine chemistry that makes the quieter moments work better than the sci-fi payoff.

ratings.plot: Tales told from the perspective of a lonely old tree standing in the middle of a botanical garden.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Enyedi's return after her Golden Bear triumph with On Body and Soul. The 147-minute runtime lets her poetic vision breathe without rushing the tree's centuries-spanning observations.

ratings.plot: A small-time reporter tries to convince the police that she saw a murder in the apartment across from hers.

verdict.reason: Essential early De Palma that showcases his Hitchcock obsessions before they became mannered. The split-screen sequences and Bernard Herrmann's final soundtrack create genuine theatrical tension that streaming can't match.
Worth seeing if you want to engage with African cinema beyond the usual festival circuit. The literary adaptation feels more suited to reading than watching.

ratings.plot:
With Some Kind of Heaven, first-time feature director Lance Oppenheim cracks the manicured facade of The Villages, America’s largest retirement community – a massive, self-contained utopia located in Central Florida. Behind the gates of this palm tree-lined fantasyland, Some Kind of Heaven invests in the dreams and desires of a small group of Villages residents – and one interloper – who are unable to find happiness within the community’s pre-packaged paradise. With strikingly composed cinematography, this
candy-colored documentary offers a tender and surreal look at the never-ending quest for finding meaning and love in life’s final act.

verdict.reason: This debut documentary delivers a sharp takedown of manufactured American paradise that belongs on a big screen. Oppenheim's candy-colored cinematography turns Florida retirement kitsch into something genuinely surreal and beautiful.

ratings.plot: A free-spirited woman "kidnaps" a yuppie for a weekend of adventure. But the fun quickly takes a dangerous turn when her ex-convict husband shows up.

verdict.reason: Demme at his most unhinged deserves the full theatrical experience. The tonal shifts from screwball to thriller work best when you cannot pause or look away.

ratings.plot: In an alternate present-day version of Oakland, telemarketer Cassius Green discovers a magical key to professional success, propelling him into a universe of greed.

verdict.reason: Riley's debut feature delivers one of the wildest third acts in recent memory, transforming workplace satire into full-blown surreal nightmare. The film's visual inventiveness and commitment to its own bizarre logic makes it essential big-screen viewing.

ratings.plot: During a three-day heat wave just before a huge 4th of July celebration, an action star stricken with amnesia meets up with a porn star who is developing her own reality TV project, and a policeman who holds the key to a vast cons...

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in ambitious failure and prophetic satire. Kelly's bloated vision of America perfectly captures our current moment, making this the rare disaster that improves with time.
Worth it if you want to discover emerging voices in experimental cinema. Torres brings a fresh perspective to the kind of personal documentary work that benefits from theatrical focus.

movies.title: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu

ratings.plot: Once a lone bounty hunter, Mandalorian Din Djarin and his apprentice Grogu embark on an exciting new Star Wars adventure.

verdict.reason: Only worth seeing if you need your Grogu fix on the biggest screen possible. Favreau directs competently but this feels like an extended TV episode that costs twenty dollars instead of your Disney Plus subscription.

movies.title: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu HDR by Barco

ratings.plot: STAR WARS: THE MANDALORIAN AND GROGU contains several sequences with flashing lights that may affect those who are susceptible to photosensitive epilepsy or have other photosensitivities. The evil Empire has fallen, and Imperial warlords remain scattered throughout the galaxy. As the fledgling New Republic works to protect everything the Rebellion fought for, they have enlisted the help of legendary Mandalorian bounty hunter Din Djarin (Pedro Pascal) and his young apprentice Grogu.

verdict.reason: Wait for reviews before committing to this franchise extension. The HDR presentation offers superior image quality, but two hours feels long for what amounts to a TV spinoff stretched to feature length.

ratings.plot: A young beautician, newly arrived in a small Louisiana town, finds work at the local salon, where a small group of women share a close bond of friendship, and welcome her into the fold.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you need a reliable tearjerker with strong ensemble work. Julia Roberts gives her breakout performance before becoming a movie star.

ratings.plot:
Selected by the Short Films Branch



For many years, author Stephen King had a “Dollar Baby Program” that would allow aspiring filmmakers to option his stories for adaptation for only one dollar. This event brings together several shorts created under the program: Paranoid (2000) is about two young women coping with an alcohol blackout; Disciples of the Crow (1983) explores a crow-worshipping cult whose members are children; Dedication (2021) tells of a Portuguese worker at a Berlin hotel and the lengths she will go to for her child; Popsy (2019) reveals a twisted gambling debt that leads to a child abduction; Beachworld (2019) portrays two astronauts struggling to survive on an alien world; and If You Tell Your Dreams... (2013) depicts a middle-aged couple and the memory of a... recent nightmare.



PROGRAM INCLUDES:



Paranoid DIRECTED BY: Jay Holben. WITH: Tonya Ivey. 2000. 8 min. USA. Color. Scope. English. Not Rated.



Dedication DIRECTED/WRITTEN BY: Selina Sondermann. WITH: Raquel Villar, Danielle Schneider, Buenaventura Braunstein, Carlos Lobo. 2021. 16 min. Germany. Color. English/German. Not Rated.



Popsy DIRECTED/WRITTEN BY: Jon Mann. WITH: Gary Belding, Kirk Belding, Charley Moore. 2019. 11 min. Canada. Color. English. Not Rated.



Beachworld DIRECTED/WRITTEN BY: Jackie Perez. WITH: Samantha Cutaran, Tom McCafferty, Lyn Ross. 2019. 14 min. USA. Color. Scope. English. Not Rated.



If You Tell Your Dreams... DIRECTED/WRITTEN BY: Yonatan Weinstein. WITH: Eli Danker, Ayelet Margalit. 2013. 7 min. Israel. Color. Hebrew. Not Rated.
ratings.director: Jay Holben, Selina Sondermann, Jon Mann, Jackie Perez, Yonatan Weinstein

verdict.reason: Skip past the expected horror beats and see rare adaptations that most King fans have never encountered. These six shorts from the author's low-budget program offer genuine discoveries spanning three decades of underground filmmaking.

ratings.plot: In 1979 Ohio, several youngsters (Elle Fanning, Joel Courtney, Gabriel Basso) are making a zombie movie with a Super-8 camera. In the midst of filming, the friends witness a horrifying train derailment and are lucky to escape with their lives. They soon discover that the catastrophe was no accident, as a series of unexplained events and disappearances soon follows. Deputy Jackson Lamb (Kyle Chandler), the father of one of the kids, searches for the terrifying truth behind the crash.

verdict.reason: Skip it unless you need nostalgic comfort food on a big screen. Abrams delivers polished Spielberg pastiche that works better as childhood memory than adult viewing experience.

ratings.plot: After getting out of prison, small-time crook Mardar stumbles upon a woman who looks exactly like his long-lost lover.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone serious about contemporary Chinese cinema or digital filmmaking as art form. Lou Ye's handheld DV camerawork turns Shanghai's polluted waterway into a fever dream of mirrors and doubles.

ratings.plot: When Danny and Ludwig meet in a leisure park, they can hardly believe their eyes: not only do they look very similar, they are the same age. Their lives are otherwise very different, so the two boys decide to exchange places.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you enjoy low-key European comedies about identity swapping. Florian Ross keeps the 87-minute runtime tight for this body-double premise.

movies.title: Teo Hernández: A Journal Is a Measure of Semen in a Die
Teo Hernández: Cluster of Eyes—Michel Nedjar, Jakobois, and MétroBarbèsRochechou Art
Teo Hernández: The Jaguar Speaks the Language of Flowers

ratings.plot: Test Film

verdict.reason: Skip this placeholder with zero actual content. The 90-minute runtime is the only real information available.

movies.title: That Time I Got Reincarnated as a Slime the Movie: Tears of the Azure Sea

ratings.plot: Rimuru and friends visit Celestial Emperor Hermesia's resort island after the Jura-Tempest Federations opening ceremony. During their vacation, they encounter a mysterious woman, leading to a new incident by the azure sea.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you're already invested in Rimuru's world, but this feels like an extended TV episode. The theatrical experience adds little beyond bigger visuals for what amounts to vacation-themed filler between major story arcs.

movies.title: That Time I Got Reincarnated as a Slime the Movie: Tears of the Azure Sea (Dubbed)

ratings.plot: Presented in an English dub After concluding the opening ceremony of the Demon Kingdom Federation Tempest, Rimuru and his companions are invited by the Celestial Emperor Hermesia of the great elven nation – the Magi Dynasty Salion – to visit her private resort island. As the group enjoys their brief vacation, a mysterious woman named Yura appears. A new incident unfolds against the backdrop of the boundless azure sea.

verdict.reason: Worth the trip if you need your anime fix on the big screen tonight. The English dub makes this accessible to casual viewers, but the beach vacation premise feels like filler between seasons.

movies.title: That Time I Got Reincarnated as a Slime the Movie: Tears of the Azure Sea (Subtitled)

ratings.plot: Presented in Japanese with English subtitles After concluding the opening ceremony of the Demon Kingdom Federation Tempest, Rimuru and his companions are invited by the Celestial Emperor Hermesia of the great elven nation – the Magi Dynasty Salion – to visit her private resort island. As the group enjoys their brief vacation, a mysterious woman named Yura appears. A new incident unfolds against the backdrop of the boundless azure sea.

verdict.reason: Skip this theatrical anime spinoff unless you are deep into the franchise. The vacation island premise feels like filler stretched to feature length rather than essential viewing.

movies.title: The Amazing Circus: The Last Act

ratings.plot: The viral internet sensation “The Amazing Digital Circus” comes to its long-awaited conclusion in a feature-length theatrical release! This “Last Act” combines episode 8 with the all-new, hour-long episode 9 for fans to see before anyone else in the world. With Caine gone and the circus dark, the cast are left with only the mistakes and traumas of their pasts to keep them company. As the prospect of eternity closes in around them, they discover the truth about the Digital Circus and its history. Will they come to terms with what they uncover, or will they make…the other choice? Also, presumably at some point, someone says something funny, because this ending can’t be THAT depressing, can it?

verdict.reason: Only worth the trip if you are already invested in this digital animation series. The feature-length conclusion combines two episodes for theatrical exclusivity, but this feels designed for existing fans rather than newcomers.

movies.title: The Best Years of Our Lives

ratings.plot: Fred, Al and Homer are three World War II veterans facing difficulties as they re-enter civilian life. Fred (Dana Andrews) is a war hero who, unable to compete with more highly skilled workers, has to return to his low-wage soda jerk job. Bank executive Al (Fredric March) gets into trouble for offering favorable loans to veterans. After losing both hands in the war, Homer (Harold Russell) returns to his loving fiancée, but must struggle to adjust.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's most honest treatments of trauma and homecoming. Wyler's direction and Gregg Toland's deep-focus photography create a visual language that makes every frame feel lived-in and real.

ratings.plot: Jeff "The Dude" Lebowski, mistaken for a millionaire of the same name, seeks restitution for his ruined rug and enlists his bowling buddies to help get it.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for the Coens at their most quotable and casually brilliant. The film's cult status exists because every rewatch reveals new details in the meticulous production design and Roger Deakins' bowling alley cinematography.

ratings.plot: In the sun-drenched expanse of the New Mexico desert lies The Boroughs, a picturesque retirement community promising its residents the time of their lives. But for new arrival Sam Cooper, paradise feels more like a prison. Everything changes when a terrifying nighttime encounter reveals that something monstrous is stalking the manicured cul-de-sacs. Dismissed by the powers that be as just another confused old man, Sam finds unlikely allies in a band of neighborhood misfits: a sharp-witted former journalist, a spiritual seeker, a cynical music manager, and a brilliant doctor running out of options. Overlooked and underestimated, these unlikely heroes must band together to unravel the dark truth at the heart of The Boroughs before their time runs out.

verdict.reason: Skip this generic retirement community horror that offers nothing you haven't seen in better films. The 75-minute runtime suggests thin material stretched to feature length.

ratings.plot: A supermom lands a Shark Tank deal, switching roles with her breadwinner husband. He struggles to adapt as a stay-at-home dad to their three daughters.

verdict.reason: Generic role-reversal comedy relies on tired gender stereotypes without fresh insight. Eric Appel brings no distinctive voice to well-worn family dysfunction material.

ratings.plot: Photographer Robert Kincaid wanders into the life of housewife Francesca Johnson for four days in the 1960s.

verdict.reason: Eastwood's mature romance plays better at home where the intimacy feels less exposed. Meryl Streep's Iowa housewife accent is the main reason to see it theatrically.

ratings.plot: Five teens visit a remote cabin and explore its cellar, finding strange items. When Dana reads from a book there, she unleashes zombie killers. But the situation is more complex than it appears.

verdict.reason: Clever horror deconstruction that works but is not urgent viewing. Goddard's meta-commentary plays fine at home and the scares lose punch on repeat viewings.

ratings.plot: From genius filmmaker Jim Henson comes The Dark Crystal, a masterful live-action fantasy starring some of the most imaginative creatures ever put on film. Many years ago, the mysterious Crystal was cracked and an age of chaos began, led by the evil race of Skeksis who rule over the land of Thra. Meanwhile, raised in solitude by a gentle race of creatures called the Mystics, the Gelfling orphan Jen, is sent on a quest to find the missing shard that will heal the Dark Crystal and restore balance to the universe.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Henson's groundbreaking puppetry that creates a fully realized fantasy world without CGI. The practical effects and creature work remain unmatched forty years later, demanding the biggest screen possible.

ratings.plot: Jack and Judy are husband and wife, and Howard is Judys father. They live in some fictional undemocratic and repressive country, and tell us a story about their lives, mostly from Jack's point of view.

verdict.reason: David Hare's stage adaptation works better as theater than cinema. The static three-person monologue structure offers little that film can enhance.

ratings.plot: Follows Miranda Priestly's struggle against Emily Charlton, her former assistant turned rival executive, as they compete for advertising revenue amidst declining print media while Miranda nears retirement.

verdict.reason: A solid sequel that lacks the original's bite but delivers enough workplace comedy to justify the ticket price. Streep elevates familiar material with a more vulnerable take on Miranda, though the fashion industry satire feels less urgent twenty years later.

movies.title: The Devil Wears Prada 2 HDR by Barco

ratings.plot: THE DEVIL WEARS PRADA 2 contains several sequences with flashing lights that may affect those who are susceptible to photosensitive epilepsy or have other photosensitivities. Twenty years after making their iconic turns as Miranda, Andy, Emily, and Nigel, Meryl Streep, Anne Hathaway, Emily Blunt, and Stanley Tucci return to the fashionable streets of New York City and the sleek offices of Runway Magazine in the eagerly awaited sequel to the 2006 phenomenon that defined a generation.

verdict.reason: Only worth it if you need the full Broadway-sized spectacle of Streep commanding a room again. The HDR format promises to make every fashion detail pop, but twenty-year-later sequels rarely justify their existence beyond nostalgia.

ratings.plot: Two parallel stories about two identical women; one living in Poland, the other in France. They don't know each other, but their lives are nevertheless profoundly connected.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's most mysterious and beautiful films about connection across distance. Kieślowski's use of color and light creates images that linger long after the credits roll.

ratings.plot: A happily-engaged couple is put to the test when an unexpected turn sends their wedding week off the rails.

verdict.reason: Borgli delivers sharp relationship comedy but the premise wears thin over 105 minutes. His knack for awkward domestic situations saves what could have been standard wedding chaos.

ratings.plot: Returning to a hotel now haunted by its mysterious past, an artist and her elderly mother confront long-buried secrets in their former family home.

verdict.reason: Hogg's ghost story rewards patient viewers but moves at her characteristic glacial pace. Tilda Swinton's dual performance anchors an otherwise deliberately opaque narrative.
Worth it for animation historians but too narrow for a general night out. The archival footage showcases Max Fleischer's pre-Code ingenuity before sanitization neutered the character.

ratings.plot: A look through the life and work of Ghanaian filmmaker Chris Hesse.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in African cinema and the power of documentary storytelling. Proudfoot brings his signature intimate approach to profiling a filmmaker whose work deserves wider recognition.
Only essential if you are tracking international arthouse releases. The premise is stronger than the execution and the runtime drags.

ratings.plot: Teenage Amy Harper, her boyfriend Buzz Dawson, and their friends Richie Atterbury and Liz Duncan visit a local carnival for a night of innocent amusement, but soon witness a fortune teller's murder and find that the exits are locked.

verdict.reason: Tobe Hooper's carnival horror belongs in a theater where the practical effects and Dean Cundey's cinematography can work their magic. The funhouse itself becomes a genuine maze of dread on the big screen.

ratings.plot: In order to save their home from foreclosure, a group of misfits set out to find a pirate's ancient valuable treasure.

verdict.reason: This is essential big-screen adventure filmmaking that rewards theatrical viewing. Donner builds genuine danger and wonder through practical effects and locations that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: A comic book artist loses his hand, which in turn takes on a murderous life of its own.

verdict.reason: Stone's early horror work shows genuine psychological insight beneath the B-movie premise. Caine's committed performance elevates what could have been pure schlock into something genuinely unsettling.

ratings.plot: In 1930s Korea, a girl is hired as a handmaiden to a Japanese heiress who lives a secluded life on a countryside estate. But the maid has a secret: She is a pickpocket recruited by a swindler to help seduce the Lady and steal her ...

verdict.reason: Park Chan-wook's intricate thriller rewards the big screen treatment with its layered production design. The film's multiple reveals benefit from the immersive focus that only theatrical viewing provides.

ratings.plot: Presented in Korean and Japanese with English subtitles From master filmmaker Park Chan-wook (OLDBOY, STOKER, LADY VENGEANCE) comes a gripping and sensual tale of two women – a young Japanese Lady living on a secluded estate, and a Korean woman who is hired to serve as her new handmaiden, but is secretly plotting with a conman to defraud her of a large inheritance. Inspired by the novel Fingersmith by British author Sarah Waters, THE HANDMAIDEN borrows the most dynamic elements of its source material and combines it with Park’s singular vision to create an unforgettable viewing experience.

verdict.reason: This is essential Park Chan-wook at his most visually sumptuous and narratively twisted. The film's labyrinthine structure rewards the big screen experience with every meticulous frame composition.

ratings.plot: After hitting something with her car, a bourgeois Argentine woman's life slowly descends into paranoia and isolation, as she fears she may have killed someone.

verdict.reason: One of the essential films about guilt and class anxiety from Argentina's finest director. Martel's sound design creates a disorienting psychological landscape that only works in a theater.

ratings.plot: At a barbecue, Ryan's five-year-old son briefly goes missing. Ryan's violent reaction to this scare sets off a chain of events and unearths secrets from the past. A journey of self-discovery and forgiveness begins.

verdict.reason: Hamdulay stretches a short film premise to feature length without earning the expansion. The thriller elements feel forced onto what wants to be a family drama.

ratings.plot: In a corrupt, greed-fueled world, a powerful alchemist leads a messianic character and seven materialistic figures to the Holy Mountain, where they hope to achieve enlightenment.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Jodorowsky's most accessible surreal masterpiece. The alchemical imagery and practical effects create a hallucinatory experience that demands the big screen.
Worth it for completists of the genre but not essential viewing tonight. The execution is competent without being memorable.
Only worthwhile if you are diving deep into Korean action cinema history. The film delivers on its modest promises without transcending them.

movies.title: The Last One for the Road

ratings.plot: Two blustery 50-somethings, have an obsession: going for their last drink. One night, they run into Giulio, a shy architecture student, whose way of seeing the world and love will be transformed as the trio wander from bar to bar ...

verdict.reason: This Italian bar-hopping character study has warmth but lacks the urgency for tonight's ticket. Sossai keeps the wandering focused enough that the 98-minute runtime never drags.

ratings.plot: A getaway driver comes out of retirement to pull off one last run - one that could send him to an early grave instead.

verdict.reason: Skip this forgettable 1971 crime thriller despite the legendary directors attached. The troubled production shows in every frame, with Huston replacing Fleischer mid-shoot and the seams visible throughout.

ratings.plot: An ambitious young journalist uncovers the horrific slaughter of twenty-two thousand Polish officers during World War II. A secret that has been kept hidden for far too many years.

verdict.reason: Skip this well-intentioned but clumsy historical drama about the Katyn massacre. The film buries its important subject under generic thriller mechanics and wooden dialogue.

ratings.plot: When Fatima leaves her close-knit suburban family to study philosophy in Paris, she finds herself caught between her religious upbringing and the freedom of student life in the city.

verdict.reason: Worth the trip for a fresh take on family and identity conflicts. Herzi directs with the specificity of someone who lived this exact tension between tradition and intellectual freedom.

ratings.plot: Private eye Philip Marlowe helps friend Terry Lennox out of a jam and is implicated in his wife Sylvia's murder. He also is hired by Eileen Wade to locate her dipsomaniac husband Roger, who frequently disappears when he wants to d...

verdict.reason: Essential Altman that belongs on the big screen where his overlapping dialogue and wide compositions can breathe. Gould's mumbling, chain-smoking Marlowe is the perfect vehicle for the director's cynical demolition of genre conventions.

ratings.plot: When a single mother and her two sons move to the sleepy seaside California town of Santa Carla, they discover much more than they anticipated in this visually stunning blend of hip humor, horror and rock 'n' roll about the most compelling group of contemporary vampires ever to put fang to vein.

verdict.reason: Worth the trip for one of the best vampire films of the 80s. Schumacher's neon-soaked Santa Carla and Kiefer Sutherland's magnetic performance make this essential big-screen viewing.

ratings.plot: The story is set in the 1970s during the period of military dictatorship. Schools were frequently closed down and society seemed to face bleak prospects on all fronts. Nonetheless, Byung Tae, a college student, enjoys pursuing rom...

verdict.reason: Ha Gil-jong captures the absurdity of life under military rule with sharp comic timing. The 1970s setting gives weight to what would otherwise be standard campus comedy.

movies.title: The Mystery of Picasso + Rembrandt: Painter of Men

verdict.reason: Clouzot's documentary captures Picasso creating art in real time, making it essential viewing for anyone interested in the creative process. The double bill format lets you compare two master filmmakers tackling artistic genius.

ratings.plot: A woman planning to testify against the mob must be protected against potential assassins on the train trip from Chicago to Los Angeles.

verdict.reason: Essential noir that earns its reputation as one of the tightest thrillers ever made. Fleischer transforms a single train into a masterclass of spatial tension where every car becomes a potential death trap.
Skip this title with zero production details or cast information. The complete absence of basic film data makes any recommendation impossible.

ratings.plot: A wealthy businessman brings his handsome male secretary/secret lover to his home, who leads the family into a maelstrom of jealousy and desire.

verdict.reason: Ha Gil-jong turns domestic melodrama into genuinely unsettling psychological territory. The 85-minute runtime keeps the hothouse atmosphere from becoming oppressive.

ratings.plot: Every year the Florida government invites the public to compete in an invasive python removal contest in the Everglades. For 10 nights, an eclectic group of hunters confront the dangerous terrain, nocturnal creatures and their own...

verdict.reason: Xander Robin's documentary has enough Florida weirdness to entertain nature lovers. The Everglades setting and eccentric hunters provide visual spectacle that works better on a big screen than at home.

ratings.plot: Every night a shepherd reads aloud a murder mystery, pretending his sheep can understand. When he is found dead, the sheep realize at once that it was a murder and think they know everything about how to go about solving it.

verdict.reason: Illumination-style animation wrapped around a premise too cute for its own good. Balda's direction feels assembly-line despite the quirky murder mystery angle.

ratings.plot: The Story of Everything takes us on a journey into the origins of humanity and the cosmos, illuminating mankind's most important scientific discoveries and revealing that everything has been created by a designer.

verdict.reason: Creationist documentary disguised as science undermines genuine cosmological wonder. The film's predetermined conclusions make for dishonest inquiry into legitimate scientific questions.

ratings.plot: A ride-share driver is thrown into her worst nightmare when she picks up a stranger.

verdict.reason: Skip this generic thriller that wastes a solid setup. Prasad never builds real suspense beyond the obvious ride-share paranoia.

ratings.plot: Mario ventures into space, exploring cosmic worlds and tackling galactic challenges far from the familiar Mushroom Kingdom.
ratings.director: Aaron Horvath, Michael Jelenic, Pierre Leduc

verdict.reason: Another disposable Nintendo cash grab with nothing to justify the theater trip. The cosmic setting feels like a cheap excuse to recycle familiar beats from the first film.

ratings.plot: A spell of time of a rural family's slightly surreal life.

verdict.reason: Charming but minor Ishii that works better as comfort viewing than essential cinema. The whimsical tone stretches thin over nearly two and a half hours.

ratings.plot: A research team in Antarctica is hunted by a shape-shifting alien that assumes the appearance of its victims.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Carpenter's masterclass in paranoid tension and practical effects wizardry. Rob Bottin's grotesque creature work remains unmatched in horror cinema and demands the biggest screen possible.

ratings.plot: Pulp novelist Holly Martins travels to shadowy, postwar Vienna, only to find himself investigating the mysterious death of an old friend, Harry Lime.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing on the big screen where Reed's tilted camera angles and deep focus photography can work their full magic. The zither music and Vienna's bombed-out sewers create an atmosphere that streaming cannot match.

ratings.plot: A wife questions her life choices as she travels to Stockholm to see her husband receive the Nobel Prize for Literature.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Glenn Close's controlled performance, but the theatrical experience adds little to what streams just as well at home. Close delivers decades of suppressed rage through micro-expressions that work better in close-up than from theater seats.
Skip this muddled political drama that fails to make its Russian power games compelling. The narrative never finds a clear perspective on its ambitious subject matter.

ratings.plot: An Arkansas waitress and a housewife shoot a rapist and take off in a '66 Thunderbird.

verdict.reason: Landmark feminist road movie that still packs a punch three decades later. Scott's direction turns the final act into genuine American mythology.

ratings.plot:
Directed by Ridley Scott from an Oscar®-winning screenplay by Callie Khouri, "Thelma & Louise" has been hailed as one of the best road movies of all time! Susan Sarandon and Geena Davis star as accidental outlaws on a desperate flight across the Southwest. With a determined detective (Harvey Keitel) on their trail, a sweet-talking hitchhiker (Brad Pitt) in their path and a string of crimes in their wake, their journey alternates between high-speed thrill ride and empowering personal odyssey.

Presented by Park Circus. THELMA & LOUISE has been digitally restored from a new 4K 16 bit scan, supervised by director Ridley Scott using the original negative. The film has been restored by The Criterion Collection with participation from MGM Studios and Park Circus

verdict.reason: This is essential viewing for one of the defining American road movies, now restored to its full cinematic power. The new 4K restoration supervised by Ridley Scott himself reveals details in Hans Zimmer's landscape cinematography that streaming cannot match.

movies.title: Tie Me Up, Tie Me Down!

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want the full-throttle fighter jet spectacle on the biggest screen possible. The aerial sequences still deliver genuine thrills that streaming flattens into nothing.

ratings.plot: A class of elite, hotshot Navy pilots at the Fighter Weapons School known as Top Gun push their aircraft and each other to the limits as they compete to be the best of the best. Tom Cruise stars as Pete "Maverick" Mitchell in the iconic 1986 blockbuster that defined a generation.

verdict.reason: See it on the biggest screen you can find for the full Reagan-era spectacle. The aerial sequences remain unmatched in their visceral impact and demand theatrical presentation.

ratings.plot: The story involves Maverick confronting his past while training a group of younger Top Gun graduates, including the son of his deceased best friend, for a dangerous mission.

verdict.reason: Skip the streaming version and see this in theaters where it belongs. Kosinski shoots the aerial sequences with practical effects that make IMAX screens feel like cockpit windows.

ratings.plot: Danny Boyle's explosive film tracks the misadventures of young men in Edinburgh trying to find their way out of joblessness, aimless relationships, and drug addiction. Some are successful, while others hopelessly are not. Based on Irvine Walsh's novel, TRAINSPOTTING melds grit with poetry, resulting in a film of harsh truths and stunning grace.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Boyle's kinetic direction and the Choose Life monologue that still hits like a freight train. The 35mm print makes Underworld's techno soundtrack feel like it's pumping through your veins.

ratings.plot: Secret Service agents make a deal with a counterfeiting inmate to be released on early parole if he will help them recover some bogus moneymaking plates, but he plans to double cross them.

verdict.reason: Richard Fleischer's noir delivers solid B-movie thrills for genre completists. The counterfeiting plot and Lloyd Bridges performance work fine theatrically, though nothing here transcends its modest ambitions.

ratings.plot: Once Yue, being still a young schoolgirl, was subjected to brutal rape on the roof of her own school. Slightly matured, she became a prostitute and began to cruelly avenge all members of the masculine gender, for her sadistic plea...

verdict.reason: Skip this exploitation exercise that mistakes shock for substance. Satô's hour-long revenge narrative offers nothing beyond its provocative premise that you cannot get from better films in the same vein.

ratings.plot: Mathias, a virtuoso pianist, lives an impossible love story.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you connect with Desplechin's restless emotional style. The director's trademark mix of literary dialogue and handheld intimacy gives weight to what could have been another doomed musician romance.

ratings.plot: In summer, Nagisa and Natsuo meet seaside exchanging distant looks and clumsy words in the rainy ocean. In winter, screenwriter Li arrives at a snowy village meeting Benzo at his guesthouse. Despite disconnected talks they embark ...

verdict.reason: Insufficient information available to make a strong recommendation either way. Without knowing the director, runtime, or basic premise, the theatrical value remains unclear.

ratings.plot: Two titles are on the line when UFC returns to Prudential Center in Newark, New Jersey, on Saturday, May 9, for UFC 328. In the main event, undefeated champion Khamzat Chimaev defends the middleweight title against former titleholder Sean Strickland for the first time since winning it last August. In the co-main event, flyweight champion Joshua “The Fearless” Van defends his title against Japanese contender Tatsuro “The Best” Taira in the first UFC championship fight between competitors born in the 21st century.

verdict.reason: Skip this unless you need the live crowd energy for MMA. The three-hour runtime punishes casual viewers when the card lacks marquee names beyond the title fights.

ratings.plot: Utamaro is a great artist who lives to create portraits of beautiful women, using the brothels of Tokyo to provide his models.

verdict.reason: Essential Mizoguchi showcasing his mastery of camera movement and composition. The brothel setting allows for some of cinema's greatest long takes.

ratings.plot: The final sixty-seven days of Van Gogh's life are examined.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Pialat's uncompromising final portrait of artistic madness and the weight of creation. Jacques Dutronc delivers a magnetic performance that captures Van Gogh's fevered energy without romanticizing his suffering.

ratings.plot: New York actors rehearse Chekhov's "Uncle Vanya" in a dilapidated theatre.

verdict.reason: Malle's final film captures theatrical magic that streaming cannot replicate. Wallace Shawn and Andre Gregory's decade-long Chekhov workshop creates unrepeatable intimacy.

ratings.plot: Three hoodlums carefully case a small town while planning to rob the bank on the upcoming Saturday. On Saturday, things turn violent and deadly.

verdict.reason: Fleischer transforms a simple heist setup into a masterclass of tension and character study. The CinemaScope photography makes every frame feel claustrophobic despite the wide open desert setting.

ratings.plot: Three lonely young denizens of Taipei unknowingly share an apartment used for sexual trysts.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's most uncompromising voices at his finest. Tsai Ming-liang's mastery of silence and space creates an urban loneliness so precise it becomes transcendent.
Worth seeing if you want to understand Korean urban development through personal lens. The documentary approach lacks the urgency needed for theatrical viewing.
See Aster's horror debut in a theater where the sound design can properly terrorize you. The film's domestic nightmare requires the kind of immersive experience that home viewing cannot match.

ratings.plot: A watch salesman meets a young woman soon leaving for Paris and becomes infatuated, so he begins to change all the clocks in Taipei to Paris time.

verdict.reason: Essential Tsai Ming-liang at his most accessible, perfect for the big screen. The static long takes and minimal dialogue create a hypnotic rhythm that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: When Nigeria Happens follows a group of misfit dancers,Fagbo, Pokko, Lighter, Movement, Colos, and Poppy,who live for their art and defy society's expectations. Their world is shaken when Fagbo's mother falls critically ill, leavi...

verdict.reason: Edosio has real affection for her characters but the 120-minute runtime tests patience. The dance sequences work better than the family crisis that drives the plot.

ratings.plot: Eight-year-old Ahmed has mistakenly taken his friend Mohammad's notebook. He wants to return it, or else his friend will be expelled from school. The boy determinedly sets out to find Mohammad's home in the neighbouring village.

verdict.reason: Kiarostami's breakthrough transforms a simple premise into profound cinema. The Iranian landscape and natural light create images that lose impact on small screens.

ratings.plot: The surveyor Giuseppe Di Noi experiments the troubles of the Italian Justice System of the '70s while back in Italy for a vacation.

verdict.reason: Skip this forgotten satire unless you have a specific interest in 1970s Italian bureaucratic comedy. Loy's direction lacks the bite needed to make this justice system critique land with contemporary audiences.

ratings.plot: N/A

verdict.reason: Aljafari's latest work likely demands theatrical viewing for full impact. Without more information about the specific film, the director's reputation suggests experimental documentary worth seeing once.

ratings.plot: After her werewolf lover unexpectedly dies in an accident, a young woman must find ways to raise their werewolf son and daughter while keeping their trait hidden from society.

verdict.reason: Hosoda's animation achieves rare emotional depth that benefits from theatrical immersion. The hand-drawn sequences of rural life contain visual poetry that streaming cannot match.
Skip this obscure thriller that never found its footing. The runtime drags without the compelling central performance needed to carry such thin material.

Worth seeing if you're drawn to Kim Ki-young's psychological extremes, but this feels minor compared to his later masterworks. The black-and-white cinematography captures the claustrophobic domestic space with striking geometric precision.

Essential viewing for Jia Zhangke's breakthrough debut that launched sixth-generation Chinese cinema. The handheld DV photography captures working-class displacement with an intimacy that established his entire aesthetic approach.
Worth a shot if you're curious about obscure international cinema. The title alone suggests this will be either brilliantly meta or completely pretentious.

ratings.plot: When a woman crashes at an empty Italian villa, posing as the owner's fiancée, she discovers an unexpected romance that could transform her life.

verdict.reason: Generic rom-com formula that streaming was invented for. The Italian scenery is the only draw, but not worth the ticket price for obvious plotting.
